coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots sensitized 2D-2D heterojunction of BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 for visible light photocatalytic removal towards the broad-spectrum antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Le, Shukun
Li, Wenjing
Wang, Yuanjiang
Jiang, Xue
Yang, Xiaoxue
Wang, Xiaojing
coredata.dc:description: Focused on the removal of the complicated residual antibiotic in aqueous environment, in this work, a novel carbon dots (C-dots) sensitized 2D-2D heterojunction of BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 were assembled through a simple hydrothermal process. The characteristic by TEM, SEM, and XPS confirmed C-dots evenly anchored on the surface of BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 heterojunction. The as-prepared C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 showed superior performance for the degradation of the various antibiotics under visible light illumination. When the concentration of C-dots in the composite is 3 wt.%, the photodegraded rates are obtained to be 91.7%, 89.3%, 87.1%, for tetracycline (TC), amoxicillin (AMX) and ciprofloxacin (CIP), respectively, without significant deactivation during consecutive ten recycle experiments. Furthermore, by assessing the antibiotics mixture solution of TC, AMX and CIP, it is proposed that the prepared samples are potentially effective for the wastewater effluents. A probable mechanism was reasonably proposed. The improved photocatalytic activities could be attributed to the unique construction of the C-dots mediated heterojunction, which could expedite electron migration, improve light harvesting capacity and enhance charge separation efficiency. The present investigation may provide a new perspective to design C-dots mediated heterojunction which could be a potential visible-light-driven photocatalysts for the better practical applications in remediation of broad-spectrum antibiotic residues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7
Heterojunction photocatalysis
Nanocomposite
Antibiotics
Visible-light-driven

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular structures of TC, AMX and CIP.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication of C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7.
XRD (a) and FT-IR (b) spectra of the BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples.
SEM (a) and TEM (b) images of VT sample, the TEM (c) and HRTEM (d) images of 3CVT-5 sample.

XPS spectra of the BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples: (a) survey; (b) Bi 4f and (c) Ta 4f of BTO, VT and CVT samples; (d) V 2p of BVO, VT and CVT samples; (e) O 1 s and (f) C 1 s of the VT and 3CVT-5 samples.

Adsorption properties of the different samples in the dark (a); Photo-degradation efficiency of TC with different as-prepared photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (b); First-order kinetics in the presence of various samples for TC degradation (c).

(a) Recycle experiments of photocatalytic TC degradation over 3CVT-5 and VT-5 under visible light irradiation; (b) XRD patterns and (c) FT-IR spectra of the initial and used 3CVT-5 for TC photo-degradation.
Effect of pH on photocatalytic degradation of TC, AMX and CIP with 3CVT-5.
The photo-degradation rates of TC, AMX, CIP [5 mg L−1 (a), 20 mg L−1 (b) and 30 mg L−1 (c)] with various samples for 120 min under visible light irradiation.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) TOC of antibiotics mixture solution degraded under different irradiation time by 3CVT-5.
UV–vis absorption (a) and the insert is the band gap energy, Photoluminescene (b), Transient photocurrent response (c) and EIS (d) for the BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples.

ESR spectra of 3CVT-5 sample for DMPO−
O2 − (a) and DMPO−
OH (b) under the visible light irradiation.
Kinetic rate constant of TC degradation with the different light irradiation.
The possible mechanism of the CVT nanocomposite photocatalytic degrading antibiotic under visible light illumination.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots sensitized 2D-2D heterojunction of BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 for visible light photocatalytic removal towards the broad-spectrum antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Physics of Rare Earth Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Huhhot, Inner Mongolia, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Physics of Rare Earth Materials
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Focused on the removal of the complicated residual antibiotic in aqueous environment, in this work, a novel carbon dots (C-dots) sensitized 2D-2D heterojunction of BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 were assembled through a simple hydrothermal process. The characteristic by TEM, SEM, and XPS confirmed C-dots evenly anchored on the surface of BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 heterojunction. The as-prepared C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 showed superior performance for the degradation of the various antibiotics under visible light illumination. When the concentration of C-dots in the composite is 3 wt.%, the photodegraded rates are obtained to be 91.7%, 89.3%, 87.1%, for tetracycline (TC), amoxicillin (AMX) and ciprofloxacin (CIP), respectively, without significant deactivation during consecutive ten recycle experiments. Furthermore, by assessing the antibiotics mixture solution of TC, AMX and CIP, it is proposed that the prepared samples are potentially effective for the wastewater effluents. A probable mechanism was reasonably proposed. The improved photocatalytic activities could be attributed to the unique construction of the C-dots mediated heterojunction, which could expedite electron migration, improve light harvesting capacity and enhance charge separation efficiency. The present investigation may provide a new perspective to design C-dots mediated heterojunction which could be a potential visible-light-driven photocatalysts for the better practical applications in remediation of broad-spectrum antibiotic residues.

ce:section.ce:para: The contamination of human and veterinary pharmaceuticals due to the metabolic excretion, extensive release from industrial waste over recent decades has subject to an increasing threat for aquatic life and human survival environment [1,2]. The main concern lies to the subsequent widespread presence of single or multiple antibiotics in the environment which present possible and still mostly unknown physiological effects on non-target species even at trace concentrations [3,4]. Therefore focusing on the issue of antibiotic residue, the efficient and speedy water purification methods are urgently researched. The antibiotics generally possess complicated aromatic rings with high stability, biotoxicity, variable intermediates, and solubility, which are making them stand up to most chemical and biological degradation processes [5]. The semiconductor-based photocatalysis is a low cost, high efficiency and environmentally friendly strategy in the treatment of the varieties of antibiotic remains, comparing with conventional purification methods [6–8]. In view of the practical application, enormous efforts have also been contributed to develop semiconductor photocatalysts driven by inexhaustible solar light to match the requirements of the economy, maneuverability, shorter periods, and no secondary pollution.

Recently, one of Bi-containing compound, bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) as a promising n-type semiconductor has attracted widely research enthusiasm in the field of visible light photocatalysis owing to its narrow band gap (about 2.4 eV) and advantageous band edge positions [9]. Regretfully, BiVO4 subjects to insufficient electron transport and ultra-low carrier mobility (∼10–2 cm2V–1s–1) [10], which has limited its practical application in the field of photocatalysis. Another Bi-containing semiconductor, Bi3TaO7 has been widely utilized for photochemical degradation to remove organic pollutants in wastewater [11]. Similar to other tantalates, Bi3TaO7 has several advantages, including high band potential to decompose the organic contaminants, satisfactory stability under strong acid and alkaline conditions [12,13]. Unfortunately, Bi3TaO7 has a wider band gap, largely restricting its adsorption for the solar light. Moreover, Bi3TaO7 could not separate photo-generated carriers effectively and was generally synthesized by orthodox high temperature solid-state reaction, which may issue in lower photocatalytic activity [14]. Generally, single-component photocatalysts invariably face common weakness such as lower utilization of visible light and easier recombination of photo-generated charges. To address these issues, the construction of semiconductor heterostructure has been showed a high effectivity [15–17]. For instance, a visible light responsive Bi3TaO7/g-C3N4 composite showed the superior photocatalytic activity for degradation of ciprofloxacin (91%, 120 min) of which were 4 and 12.2 times higher than those of bare Bi3TaO7 and g-C3N4 [15]. Compared with single MnNb2O6 and BiOI, the hybridmaterial (NGQDs-BiOI/MnNb2O6) could significantly enhance photocatalytic activity for tetracycline (87.2%, 120 min), oxytetracyline (72.1%, 120 min), ciprofloxacin (57.4%, 120 min), and doxycycline (64.7%, 120 min) under visible light irradiation [16]. Mengxia Ji et al. integrated a 2D-2D stacking g-C3N4/Bi4O5Br2 and they found the composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance for ciprofloxacin degradation (50%, 30 min visible light irradiation), which was only about 17% for the single Bi4O5Br2 [17]. Recently, the improved photocatalytic antibiotics degradation for BiVO4-based heterojunctions were also reported, such as Fe3O4QDs@BiOCl/BiVO4 [18], BiVO4/WO3 [19], BiVO4/g-C3N4 [20], and BiVO4/Bi2Mo3O12 [21], all successfully enhancing both collection and separation efficiency of the photogenerated charges for different applications. We are particularly interested to design a 2D-2D heterojunction by coupling BiVO4 and Bi3TaO7 that has suitably aligned band structures. However, this mode is possibly unfavorable for the high stable antibiotic degradation because the resultant decreased redox potentials may not perfectly complete antibiotic oxidation [17]. In this case, it is predicted to be a better solution by introducing an electron shuttle bridge such as Au, Ag and Pt to construct an intergraded heterojunction system with two different semiconductors.

C-dots are an original class and environmentally friendly zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials which exhibits tunable strong photoluminescence (PL) emissions, excellent electrical conductivity, and promising electron reservoir properties [22–24]. Particularly, C-dots prefer a wide range of up-conversion absorption in the near IR region, and thus could serve as an excellent spectral harvester and converter [25]. When C-dots coupled with the appointed semiconductors, the nanocomposites could absorb a longer wavelength near-infrared light and then emit a shorter wavelength ultraviolet light, which allows the utilization of sun spectrum with UV, visible, and near-infrared light. Therefore, C-dots have been confirmed to be the promising mediator for catalysis to improve the photocatalytic activity by decreasing the recombination of electron-hole and broadening the photo-absorptive region. Newly, C-dots have been coupled with different semiconductors, such as ZnO [26], C3N4 [27], BiOI [28], Ag3PO4 [29], and BiVO4 [30] to improve their photocatalytic activity. Consequently, utilizing 0D C-dots as a mediated component to bridge 2D BiVO4 and Bi3TaO7 should be an advisable strategy for the promotion of the photocatalytic degradation efficiency towards the various antibiotic contaminations.

Herein, we prepared a novel C-dots modified BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 sample through a facile hydrothermal method. As shown in Fig. 1 , tetracycline (TC), amoxicillin (AMX) and ciprofloxacin (CIP), the representatives for three kind antibiotics of tetracycline, β-lactam, and quinolones [31–33], were used as models to assess the antibiotic removal performance of the as-prepared ternary sample under visible light irradiation. The mechanism for improving visible light absorption and enhancing photo-induced carriers transport was proposed based on the testing data of the UV diffuse spectra, photoelectrochemical measurements, band potential, and active species trapping. The present investigation demonstrates the ternary C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 nanocomposite is a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for better practical applications in remediation of the various antibiotic contaminants.

The chemicals utilized in this work, TaCl5 (CAS: 7721-01-9), NH4VO3 (CAS: 7803-55-6), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (CAS: 10035-06-0), HNO3 (CAS: 7697-37-2), KOH (CAS: 1310-58-3), C2H5OH (CAS: 64-17-5), (CH2OH)2 (CAS: 107-21-1), ascorbic acid (CAS: 50-81-7) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd in Shanghai, China. TC (98% in purity, CAS: 60-54-8), AMOX (98% in purity, CAS: 26787-78-0) and CIP (98% in purity, CAS: 85721-33-1) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China (for both human and livestock). Antibiotics in this work were dissolved in 1 mol L HCl with heating due to their limited solubility in water. All chemical reagents in this research are of analytical grade.

The C-dots were originally synthetized by “bottom-up” approaches which using a hydrothermal method as follows: 1.6 g ascorbic acid was dissolved in 15 mL (CH2OH)2 and 25 mL deionized water under 30 min magnetic stirring to form a homogeneous solution. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon liner of stainless steel autoclave and heated at 453 K for 4 h in an oven. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the transparent light-yellow product was centrifuged and dialyzed by using cellulose ester dialysis membranes (MW: 8000–14000) for 48 h to obtain C-dots. The C-dots solution was sealed and stored in dry and cool place for subsequent fabrication and characterization.

The fabrication strategy for C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 is illustrated in Fig. 2 . Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (1 mmol) dissolved in 20 mL HNO3 (4 mol·L−1). TaCl5 (0.16 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL C2H5OH and NH4VO3 (0.5 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL KOH (4 mol·L−1). Subsequently, NH4VO3 and TaCl5 solution were wisely dropped into Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solution under magnetic stirring. The mixture solution pH was adjusted to pH = 9 by adding KOH solution (7 mol·L−1). Next, the C-dots (0.5, 1, 3, 5 wt.%) was added into the mixture and continuously stirred for 6 h. The resultant mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon liner of stainless steel autoclave and heated at 453 K for 24 h in an oven. Then cooling down naturally, the yellow product was washed with deionized water and ethanol repeatedly, and dried at 353 K for 12 h. At last, the yellow powder was obtained. Based on the above methods, the weight ratios of BiVO4 and Bi3TaO7 were 2:8, 5:5 and 8:2 were denoted as VT-2, VT-5 and VT-8, respectively. A series of C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 ternary composites with different contents of C-dots were prepared, labeled as nCVT-5 for simplicity, where n represented the mass ratio of C-dots additive amount. For a comparative study, BiVO4 and Bi3TaO7 were prepared by similar method, denoted as BVO and BTO.

The phase of as-prepared samples was investigated by X-ray diffraction (Bruck AXS, Germany) (Cu Kα, 40 kV, 30 mA). Fourier transform near-infrared (FT-IR) analyses were recorded on a 6700 Nicolet Avatar spectrometer (Nicolet Corp., USA). A Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrum was utilized over an Al-Kα X-ray source and the C 1 s signal was set at the position of 284.6 eV. The morphology was observed on the scanning electron microscopy (S-4800 HITACHI) and transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN). The UV–vis reflectance spectra were recorded on PerkinElmer Lambda UV–vis-NIR spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) property was obtained over Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. Electrochemical analyzer (PGSTAT302 N, Metrohm Autolab) was utilized. The ESR signals were investigated by JES FA200 spectrometer. Relative parameter details of all characteristic methods used are listed in Supporting Information (SI: 1. Details of characteristic methods).

The photocatalytic activity of BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples was evaluated by degrading TC, AMX and CIP under visible light illuminating, respectively. In each experiment, 15 mg photocatalyst was added into 30 mL antibiotics solution (the concentration is 5 mg L−1, 20 mg L−1 and 30 mg L−1, respectively). Firstly, to guarantee the equalization of adsorption-desorption, the mixture was stirring 60 min in the dark. Secondly, the visible light source was a 500 W Xenon lamp. At intervals of 20 min, 5 mL solution was sampled and then centrifuged to remove the particles. Finally, to measure the absorbance of solution, UV–vis spectrometer was used. The λMAX wavelength for the different antibiotics was set to λMAX (TC) = 355 nm, λMAX (AMOX) = 226 nm, and λMAX (CIP) = 277 nm, respectively. The photo-degradation rate was calculated using the following equation:
Photo - degradation rate =
C t
C 0
=
(
A 0
- A )
A 0
× 100 %
Where C0 was the initial concentration of antibiotics solution, Ct was the concentration at time t, A0 was the initial absorbance of antibiotics solution and A was the absorbance at time t. To estimate the stability of the photocatalyst, after each experiment, the photocatalyst was collected via centrifugation, washed and dried. Then, the samples were recycled 10 runs with the same procedure. Meanwhile, the total organic carbon (TOC), total inorganic carbon (TIC) and total carbon (TC) were analysis during the experimental process and recorded by intervals of 30 min. The TOC, TIC and TC were determined by Vario EL cube (Elementar, German).

The XRD analysis was employed to explore the crystalline structure of BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples with different compositions and the results were displayed in Fig. 3 a. The characteristic XRD peaks of the as-prepared BVO correspond with the diffraction peaks of BiVO4 monoclinic phase structure (JCPDS card No. 14-0688) [34], while the detectable peaks of BTO match well with Bi3TaO7 cubic phase structure (JCPDS card No. 44-0202) [35]. Obviously, BiVO4 and Bi3TaO7 are the predominant constituents in the composite samples both VT and CVT. Additionally, the absence of significant C-dots XRD pattern in CVT, demonstrates that the C-dots modified on the surface of VT are limited concentration, lower crystallinity, higher dispersion. The similar result can also be validated in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3b). The band at 1623 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibrations of H2O molecules adsorbed, while the band at 1382 cm−1 correspond to the nitrate adsorbed owing to residual nitrate in the samples [36]. Moreover, the peaks at 872 cm−1 and 1103 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of Bi-O bond [37]. The characteristic peak at 471 cm−1 for BVO, VT and CVT is recorded as VO vibration [38]. In the IR spectra of BTO, VT and CVT, the peak at 665 cm−1 is elucidated as TaO and TaOTa vibration [39]. Distinctly, the samples FT-IR spectra indicate that the modification of C-dots in CVT is not destroyed the VT structure.

The SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of synthesized VT and 3CVT-5 are illustrated in Fig. 4 . The SEM (Fig. 4a) observation displayed that both BVO and BTO exhibited the stacked nanosheets pattern. In addition, VT was a 2D/2D structure consisting of BVO nanosheets embedded in the layered BTO. As shown in Fig. 4b, the TEM image of VT presented BTO (light grey in image) and BVO (dark grey in image) nanosheets with about 25 nm sizes which have been composited to form a heterostructure. In Fig. 4c, it could be distinctly seen that numerous of C-dots with the diameters around 10 nm were homogeneous deposited over the surface of VT heterostructure. Besides, the modification of C-dots does not expressively change the size and morphology of the VT main structure. Moreover, the TEM images of 1CVT-5 and 5CVT-5 samples (Fig. S1) also demonstrated that only a few C-dots were detected in the 1CVT-5, while the excessive amounts of C-dots stacked on the VT surface in the 5CVT-5. The HRTEM of the 3CVT-5 sample is showed in Fig. 4d. The three interplanar spaces of the ternary CVT nanocomposite, d = 0.325 nm, 0.273 nm, and 0.290 nm correspond to the (0 0 2) plane of C-dots, (2 0 0) plane of Bi3TaO7 and (0 4 0) plane of BiVO4, respectively [35,40].

The chemical compositions and elements chemical states of the BVO, BTO, VT and 3CVT-5 nanocomposites were investigated via XPS technique. The corresponding elements were all observed for BVO, BTO, VT, and CVT, respectively, in their survey spectra (Fig. 5 a–d). And the Bi 4f peaks imply the presence of Bi3+ in the crystal structure of BVO, BTO, VT and 3CVT-5 samples [41] while the peaks of Ta 4f and V 2p confirm that Ta5+ and V5+ oxidation state mainly exists in the BTO, VT and 3CVT-5 samples [35,42]. Likewise, the O 1 s spectra of VT-5 in Fig. 5e is fitted into two peaks located at 531.3 eV and 532.6 eV, which are originated from the crystal lattice oxygen and hydroxyl groups [43]. Compared with VT-5, a new peak at 533.2 eV appears in the 3CVT-5 heterojunction, which may correspond to CO groups originated water bonded on the surface [43]. Fig. 5f demonstrated the C 1 s spectrum in the VT-5 sample could be fitted to two peaks centered at 284.6 eV and 286.6 eV corresponded respectively to the CC and COH/COC [44]. For 3CVT-5 nanocomposites, a characteristic peak observed at 287.7 eV is ascribed to CO groups, in great agreement with that in the references [44]. It is speculated an existence of the interaction between C-dots and CVT.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were estimated by the photodegradation of TC under visible-light at room temperature as displayed in Fig. 6 and Fig.S2. From Fig.S2a, the pure BVO and BTO exhibit the lower TC degradation efficiency while the VT-5 displays the best visible-light photocatalytic activity with a 33.5% degradation rate. After the modification of C-dots, the TC degradation efficiency shows an outstanding enhancement, which indicate that the addition of C-dots is a competent way to improve the photocatalytic activity of VT. Among the different amount of C-dots displayed in Fig. S2b, the 3CVT-5 exhibits the highest photocatalytic TC degradation efficiency (about 85.3%) under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, it was observed that 3CVT-5 adsorbed more antibiotic organic molecules after 40 min adsorption-desorption equilibrium (Fig. 6a). Nevertheless, when the enhancement of C-dots over 3 wt.%, the removal performance is decreased. This indicates that the modification of C-dots is beneficial for the charge transfer from VT, whereas too many C-dots heaping on the surface of VT not only block the absorption of light over semiconductor but also decrease active sites. The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples is compared with TiO2 Degussa P-25 (P25) powders in Fig. 6b and the 3CVT-5 sample obviously displays the better TC photo-degradation efficiency. The degradation rate of the 3CVT-5 nanocomposite, calculated using pseudo-first-order kinetic model, is 0.0451, respectively about 4.7, 5.6, 11.9 and 17.2 times higher than those of the P25, VT-5, pure BVO and BTO samples (Fig. 6c), which clearly reveals that the modification of C-dots over the VT hybrid material has meaningfully improvement on the photocatalytic activity with the visible light driving. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared 3CVT-5 sample is much better than most of the reported photocatalysts which has been shown in Table S1.

The photocatalytic stability and reusability of the VT-5 and 3CVT-5 samples are also analyzed by the ten runs recycle experiments of TC degradation (Fig. 7 a). It can be seen the photocatalytic activity of VT-5 quickly decrease after 10 runs recycle experiments. However, 3CVT-5 sample still displays well photocatalytic stability for the ten recycles under visible light irradiation although a slightly decrease has been also observed due to the loss of the catalyst during the filtration and collection process. Obviously, the addition of C-dots could improve the photocatalytic stability and reusability performance. The XRD pattern and FT-IR spectra of the initial and used 3CVT-5 sample for TC degradation is provided in Fig. 7b and c. Obviously, the main XRD characteristic peaks of 3CVT-5 remain changeless and FT-IR spectrum also displays the peaks in the same position during 10 sequential cycles, identifying the excellent texture stability of the as prepared CVT samples.

It has been reported that the solution pH could largely influence the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics [45]. Fig. 8 shows the effect of pH on the removal of TC, AMX and CIP with 3CVT-5. The pKa values are 3.3, 7.7 and 9.3 (pKa1, pKa2, pKa3) for TC, 2.4, 7.4 and 9.4 (pKa1, pKa2, pKa3) for AMX, 6.1 and 8.7 (pKa1, and pKa2) for CIP, respectively, according to the references [15]. Interestingly, in terms of the pH-dependent relation, the pH trend on k values for TC degradation is opposite to AMX and CIP. The removal of TC is faster with increasing pH, whereas the k value notably decreases in the case of AMX and CIP degradation with increasing pH. It has been noted, in acidic media (pH < 7), TC, AMX and CIP will be protonated to their cationic forms. In opposite, TC, AMX and CIP will be dissociated to their anionic forms when the pH value of solution increased to 9. With regard to TC, it has reported that there is visible light absorption over 420 nm with the species of HTC− and TC2−, while the species of H3TC+ and H2TC only absorb the UV light below 420 nm [45], inferring a decrease of photo-decomposition in acid media. For AMX, it is easily hydrolyzed in acidic media due to the amine functional groups. M.L. Tran et al. [46] also reported that AMX is mainly degraded by hydrolysis reaction. As for CIP, the decrease of activity in alkaline media could be attributed to the negative effects from anion for their possible role of radicals quenching [47]. Under neutral media, the degradation of three antibiotics is relatively stable.

Besides, to investigate the extensive practicability for the various antibiotics, the removals of three kind antibiotics of tetracycline, β-lactam, and quinolones (TC, AMX and CIP) are researched under visible light at room temperature with a concentration range from 5, to 20 and to 30 mg L−1. The molecular structures of TC, AMX and CIP are shown in Fig. 1. It is obviously that three kinds of antibiotics have complicated aromatic rings structure with completely different molecular geometries. For 5 mg L−1 of TC, AMX and CIP catalyzed by 3CVT-5, Fig. 9 a displays the excellent photocatalytic activities of 91.7%, 89.3% and 87.1%, respectively. With the increase of antibiotics concentration from 5 mg L−1 to 20 mg L−1 and 30 mg L−1, the estimated results (Fig. 9b and c) show 3CVT-5 has the extraordinary ability to photodegrade various antibiotics under visible light illuminating.

In considering of practical application, we mixed three antibiotics of TC (20 mg L−1), AMX (20 mg L−1) and CIP (10 mg L−1) to estimate the performance of the prepared samples under the various antibiotics. The UV–vis temporal spectral changes of the antibiotics mixture solution photodegraded by 3CVT-5 under visible light illuminating are obtained (Fig. 10 a). It can be clearly seen that an intensely absorption band gradually became weaker with prolonged degradation time. After nearly 120 min of illumination, the absorption band is nearly disappeared, indicating the excellent degradation of the antibiotics mixture solution over 3CVT-5. To further prove the mineralization potentials in the antibiotics mixture solution, TOC analyses was studied with 3CVT-5 (Fig. 10b). It is clearly that the TOC decline from 31.50 to 18.33 mg L−1 while the TIC increase from 0 to 3.76 mg L−1 during the 150 min process, inferring a small quantity of inorganic compounds such as NH4 +, CO3 2− and NO3 2−, existing in the solutions. The lower TOC removal rate (41.8%) suggests that it is easier to destroy the organic group of antibiotics rather than to completely decompose it into CO2 and H2O. In addition, the steadily decrease of both Total Carbon and TOC during the photo-degradation indicated that CVT could be successfully applied to the removal of the broad-spectrum antibiotics in the practical wastewater as a kind of the promising visible photocatalytic material.

Mass spectra investigations are settled to analyze the photo-degradation products of TC (Fig. S3). Except for TC with the charge-to-mass ratio (m/z) = 445, there are about 15 kinds of byproducts detected via LC–MS technology (Fig.S3a-c). As shown in Fig.S3d, the formulas of the intermediates have been concluded though their charge-to-mass ratios (m/z = 198, 247, 274, 277, 291, 318, 329, 343, 345, 360, 362, 402, 410, 427 and 429). By combining with the LC–MS and TOC data, it is obviously that most of the TC and its intermediates being mineralized into the corresponding small organic molecules, carbon oxides, and water and inorganic ions. Most of the photo-degradation intermediates of TC have been also observed in the recited references which are considered to be less toxic and harmless [30,48].

Firstly, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) is employed to discuss the light absorption properties of the BVO, BTO, VT and CVT (Fig. 11 a). It can be seen that each sample has its own characteristic adsorption. BTO obviously absorbs with wavelengths shorter than 435 nm in UV light and BVO reveals an obvious absorption band at the region of 200–484 nm. Compared to their counterparts, an obviously red shift of the VT and CVT composite samples are observed with increased visible light absorption. The band gap energies of BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples could be estimated using the means of Kubelka-Munk theory [49]. In the inset of Fig. 9a, the band gap energies of pure BVO and BTO samples are around 2.46 eV and 2.94 eV respectively, and the value of VT is obtained as 2.39 eV. Meanwhile the introduction of C-dots gradually diminished the band gap energy of CVT, from 2.39 eV to 2.27 eV. The similar results were reported in the recited literature [30]. The results elucidate that the modification of C-dots increase light absorption region, following with the enhanced visible light catalytic ability.

Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) technique is performed to estimate the samples’ optical properties which are reflecting to the transfer and recombination of charge carrier in the system (Fig. 11b). It is observed that the pure BVO and BTO exhibited the higher emission peaks over the excitation wavelength at 315 nm. As for the VT sample, the PL peak was reduced in the same condition. With the modification of C-dots, the CVT nanocomposite presented the lowest emission intensity among the as-prepared samples. As well-known that the PL behavior is nearly related to the electron-hole pair recombination, while the lower PL intensity means the lower recombination of the charges [50]. Consequently, it can be elucidated that the modification of C-dots over the VT can remarkably enhance the photocatalytic efficiency.

C-dots are suggested to be an outstanding electron reservoir to capture photo-generated electrons and thus restrain carriers recombination [51,52]. Fig. 11c shows the photocurrent response for several on-off cycles under visible light irradiation. The pure BVO and BTO display a lower photocurrent density upon visible light irradiation while the VT shows a high photocurrent in comparison to pure BVO and BTO. As expected, the CVT sample is significantly high photocurrent under irradiation over the other samples, indicating that the modified C-dots could transfer the interfacial electrons efficiently and lead to faster electron-hole pair separation. In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to illustrate the separation and transfer of the photo-induced carries of BVO, BTO, VT and CVT samples. From Fig. 11d, it is found that the arc diameter on CVT Nyquist plot is significantly smaller than that of single or binary components (BVO, BTO and VT), which confirms that the effective separation and fast interfacial charge migration of photo-generated electron-hole pairs by introducing C-dots.

Three scavengers of holes (h+), superoxide radical (
O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) is EDTA, 1, 4-benzoquinone (BQ) and t-BuOH, respectively, which have been used to test the predominant reactive species in the photocatalytic TC degradation process [53]. It was observed that when EDTA was introduced to the TC degradation reaction, the h+ had low impression (Fig. S4). However, when BQ and t-BuOH were added into the solution to trap the
O2 − and
OH, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency obviously changed which confirming that
O2 − and
OH radicals were the predominant reactive species in the TC degradation process. To further reveal the major reactive species in the present system, the existence of
O2 − and
OH in the 3CVT-5 photocatalytic system was further proved with electron spin resonance (ESR) technique (Fig. 12 ). We found, there are no clear signals observed in the dark. Nevertheless, the notable signals of
O2 − and
OH are found in the spectra under visible light irradiation. In addition, the intensity of
O2 − and
OH is gradually increasing with the increase of the irradiation time. It indicates that in the CVT system, both
O2 − and
OH radicals is produced. These active species finally dedicate to the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic residue, consistent with the above active species trapping experiment.

The photocatalytic activity of the VT and CVT samples has also been estimated for TC degradation under four light sources. The degradation kinetic rate constants of TC are shown in Fig. 13 , where can be observed that the photocatalytic activity of 3CVT-5 is 1.09, 1.57, 5.61, and 13.29 times higher than that of VT-5 under UV light irradiation, simulated sunlight irradiation, visible light irradiation, and near-infrared light irradiation, respectively. The significantly improved photocatalytic responses in visible light and near-infrared light provide the strong evidence for the up-converted PL (UCPL) property of C-dots. Generally, C-dots cannot achieve its UCPL property effectively under short wavelength irradiation.

Moreover, the conduction band (CB) positions of the semiconductor were calculated using the Mulliken electronegativity theory [10]: ECB = X – Ee − 0.5 Eg, where X represents the semiconductor’s Mulliken electronegativity and Ee is the hydrogen scale of free electrons energy. The valence band (VB) positions is calculated by EVB = ECB + Eg [54]. The band gap energy (Eg) of pure BVO and BTO is 2.46 eV and 2.94 eV, respectively, which is based on the DRS results. Thus, the CB and VB potentials of the BVO are calculated to be + 0.31 eV and + 2.77 eV, meanwhile those of BTO is obtained to be + 0.29 eV and + 3.23 eV. K. Wang et al. reported the similar results [15]. When combining BVO and BTO to build heterojunction, in term of the alignment of their band levels, electrons and holes ﬂow all from BTO to BVO. As a result, the electron and hole pairs are quickly combined, revealing the poor catalytic performance. Thus, this binary composite is not expected to produce O2, which could be convinced by weak signals of both
O2 − and
OH species under visible irradiation in ESR test (Fig. S5). Nevertheless, by introducing C-dots, photo-generated electrons of BVO and BTO, will tend to be accumulated in the C-dots and thus the electron and holes are well separated. Clearly, the VB of BTO is more positive than that of the OH−/
OH (2.38 eV vs NHE) indicating that the h+ can be used to oxidize OH− to form the
OH radicals, meanwhile
O2 − radicals is produced through the migration of the photo-generated electrons via C-dots [48].

Highlighting by the above experimental and theoretical research, the possible mechanism for the photocatalytic antibiotic degradation over the CVT nanocomposite is speculated and displayed in Fig. 14 . First, in the integrated construction, C-dots acted as good electron acceptors, could act as an electron reservoir to make electrons transfer easily to C-dots from CB of both BVO and BTO, promoting electron-hole separation and consequently enhancing their lifetimes [43]. Secondly, C-dots could serve as the electron migration tunnel in the system, accelerating the transfer of electrons, which further inhibits the electron-hole pairs’ recombination and increases the amount of carriers to improve photocatalytic antibiotics degradation. Thirdly, due to distinctive UCPL effect of the C-dots, under visible light illumination, they first absorb visible light and subsequently longer wavelength light emission in the range of near-infrared light, which is further used by CVT to generate more electron-hole pairs. Therefore the visible light absorption of CVT is extended and the quantum efficiency is promoted. Moreover, photo-generated electrons in C-dots would be captured by O2 on surface of samples to generate active
O2 −, which is confirmed as one of the main active species in photocatalytic oxidative process. Meanwhile, holes in VB of CVT would transfer to surface of BVO and then react with OH− to produce
OH, together with h + regarded as another two major active radicals. This is also confirmed by the trapping experiment and ESR analysis. Finally, as a carbon-based material, the C-dots can adsorb the antibiotic molecules to provide more reactive sites. Therefore, with the modification of C-dots, the catalytic activity and stability is effectively increased.

In general, an economical and efficient ternary photocatalyst was fabricated by modifying the C-dots over the BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 heterojunction. The morphological, optical and electronic researches elucidated that the as prepared C-dots/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 nanocomposite displayed the remarkable photocatalytic activity for antibiotics degradation. The optimizational component proportion is 3CVT-5 with a degradation rate of 91.7%, 89.3% and 87.1% for TC, AMX and CIP, respectively, without significant deactivation during the consecutive ten-run tests. Meanwhile, the assessment of the antibiotics mixture solution confirms that our samples may be special effectively for the practical effluents. The photocatalytic mechanism was proposed based on the testing of the UV diffuse spectra, photoelectrochemical measurements, band potential, and active species trapping. The major reactive species are proved to be
O2 − and
OH radicals. It was found the improved activity can be attributed to the formation of a heterojunction and more importantly, the existence of C-dots, which could enhance light harvesting capacity, improve electron transfer and accelerate molecular oxygen activation. This research further provides a new perspective to design C-dots mediated heterojunction which could be a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for the better practical applications to remedy the hazardous antibiotic residents in aqueous environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 21777078 and 21567017), the Project of Research and Development of the Applied Technology for Inner Mongolia (201702112).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.04.088.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
